content_html:
MKT10007 Lecture 10
Dr Agung Yoga Sembada (Yoga)
Dr Gordon Campbell

Lecture 10
Branding
What is a brand
Brand elements
Brand relationships
Brand extensions

Where would you find Branding on our Marketing Process flowchart?

Branding is part of the communication the Positioning.

Origin of branding
The term has originated from an old Norse word ‘brandr’ which means ‘to burn’.
Most commonly associated with branding an emblem into a cow’s hide to identify the owner.
Branding then became used to identify a particular artisan or craftsperson’s work
Eventually organisations started using logos to make it easy for consumers to recognise their preferred brand

Part 1. What is a brand?
Brand Name
That part of a brand that can be spoken, including letters, words and numbers,
e.g. Nike
Brand Mark
The elements of a brand that cannot be spoken.
e.g. The Nike swoosh
Brand
A name, term, symbol, design or combination thereof that identifies a seller’s products and differentiates them from competitors’ products.
Branding is the action of endowing products and services with the power of the brand
LO4

Brand as promise
A brand is simply a promise of consistency. A promise of -:
Quality
Performance
Future expectations
https://jezebel.com/check-out-these-celebs-who-make-the-same-face-in-every-1554441484 Human brands
https://www.swinburne.edu.au/media/intranet/images/international-and-future-students/resources/Swinburne_Brand_Style_Guide.pdf
Every brand has a Style Guide, a manual intended to ensure that the Brand is always presented consistently. Similarly, some celebrities want to photographed looking the same every time. See here

An effective brand name …
… is easy to recognise and remember
… is easy to pronounce
… describes the product, use and benefits
… is short, distinctive and unique
… has a positive connotation
… is legally protectable
… reinforces the product image
... is translatable into other languages
LO4

3 Roles of branding
Repeat sales
New-product sales
Product identification
Benefits of branding
Strong brands have value, a concept known as brand equity. It can be measured in relation to brand awareness level, perceived quality and loyalty.
Global brands are those for which at least 20 per cent of the product is sold outside the home country.
LO4

The importance of branding
The most distinctive skill of professional marketers is their ability to create, maintain, protect and enhance brands
A branded product is one whose dimensions differentiate it from other products designed to satisfy the same need
Differences may be related to the performance of the brand, e.g. functional, rational or tangible
May be related to what brand represents – symbolic, emotional
Ultimately a brand resides in the minds of consumers

Brand vs offering
Traditionally the brand is used to support the offering
The brand functions to “teach” consumers:
“who” the product is, “what” the product does, “why” consumers should care.
But sometimes the offering takes a back seat to the power of the brand
Raises the issue of the subjectivity of value

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BJhZ0J3bIYc Josh Bell – 7 mins
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bqPARIKHbN8  Oobah Butler fake London restaurant – 18 mins
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HxXbrnJ6l4A iphone7 hoax – Jimmy Kimmel – 4 mins
Strong brands command trust and premium prices

Usefulness of brands for consumers
Knowledge of brands is increasingly apparent and important, for consumers and for marketers
Consumers accumulate knowledge about brands from past experiences, other people’s experiences and marketing activities
Brands simplify decision making and reduce risk for consumers
Consumers lives are complicated, rushed, and time-starved
Knowing which brands satisfy your needs makes it easy to be a consumer

The role of a brand
Identify the maker - allows consumer to assign responsibility for its performance
Signal quality and trustworthiness to consumers
Serve as a competitive advantage
Create barriers to entry that make it difficult for other firms to enter the market, because it is expensive to create a well-known brand
Offer legal protection for unique features or aspects of the product; Intellectual property rights via trademark, patent, copyright
http://mentalfloss.com/article/27396/9-trademarked-colors
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZGxzuxsg-0w Hyundai

Part 2. Brand elements
Brand elements are artefacts that identify and differentiate the brand
Brand elements include:
Brand names
Slogans
Characters
Logos
URLs
The test is what consumers would think or feel about the product if the brand element were all they knew
https://medium.com/@inkbotdesign/top-10-most-expensive-logo-designs-rebrands-ever-402ea80d8f0b
https://www.goldennumber.net/wp-content/uploads/pepsi-arnell-021109.pdf

Brand element choice criteria

https://www.snopes.com/fact-check/daycare-center-murals/
Read this to find out why>>

“Brand onion” principle
Brands are positioning statements, manifested – brought to life.
It starts with the essence, manifested outwards
The brand is a strategic and deliberate commitment to appeal to a segment
Even sometimes at a cost to other segments

Brand positioning
Positioning is the act of designing a company’s offering and image to occupy a distinctive place in the minds of the target market
Deciding on Brand Positioning requires:
Determining a frame of reference by identifying the target market and relevant competition
Identifying the optimal points of parity (equal to competition) and points of difference brand associations given that frame of reference
Creating a brand mantra to summarize the positioning and essence of the brand

Competitive frame of reference
The competitive frame of reference defines which other brands are being competed against
Which brands should be the focus of competitive analysis
Can examine competition from both an industry and a market point of view
An industry is a group of firms offering a product or class of products that are close substitutes for one another
Using the market approach, we define competitors as companies that satisfy the same customer need
E.g.: Netflix considers Fortnite to be their competitors more than HBO, because Fortnite is taking the attention of part of their audience – Read this >>
https://www.theverge.com/2019/3/3/18246868/attention-economy-fortnite-advertising-user-engagement

Points of difference
Attributes and the benefits they give to consumers:
strongly associate with a brand,
positively evaluate, and
believe they could not find to the same extent with a competitive brand
Strong brands may have multiple points-of-difference
Three key criteria determine whether a brand association can truly function as a point-of-difference:
Desirability to consumers – do they want it?
Deliverability by the organisation – can we do it?
Differentiability to competitors – is it different to what competitors can deliver, and can we explain to the consumers in what way are different?

Points of parity
Brands also need to establish associations that places them in the category they want to be considered
E.g. “A car needs four wheels, an engine and a steering wheel”
These types of associations come in two basic forms:
Category points-of-parity are attributes or benefits that consumers view as essential to a legitimate and credible offering within a certain product or service category
Competitive points-of-parity are associations designed to overcome perceived weaknesses of the brand,
i.e. negate competitors’ perceived points-of-difference or negate a perceived vulnerability of the brand. See below for how brands attempt to create and counter points-of-parity >>>
https://www.entrepreneur.com/article/294456   Snapchat vs Instagram
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwcr3cc0LZM Bear Grylls – Netflix game

Building brand equity
Marketers build brand equity by creating the right brand knowledge structures with the right consumers
Ways in which this can be achieved, include:
Choice of brand elements or identities making up the brand
Other associations indirectly transferred to the brand by linking it to some other entity

Another way to build brand equity is to “borrow” it >>>
Link the brand to other information in memory that conveys meaning to consumers
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCFk7dqVnBayNPbrMtIwtaSw These Masterclass YouTube videos will give you personal inspiration
Borrow your Brand equity

Leveraging other brands through alliances
Co-branding is when two established brand names of different companies are used on the same product
Offers many advantages:
Combined brands create broader consumer appeal and greater equity
Can expand into a category might otherwise have difficulty entering alone
But also has limitations:
Complicated marketing efforts
Reputational risk

Leveraging brand through endorsements
https://www.imdb.com/title/tt9412098/videoplayer/vi2031663641
These endorsers enabled a con

Part 3. Brand Relationships
Customer-based brand equity
Result of a consumer’s brand knowledge
Brand knowledge: all thoughts, feelings, images, experiences, beliefs etc. that become associated with the brand
Brand equity is reflected in perceptions, preferences and behavior related to all aspects of the marketing of a brand
Can often be seen by the premium prices some brands can charge.

CBBE Pyramid

Brand personality
To forge an intimate connection with consumers, a brand must emphasise its personality
So marketers often try to imbue brands with human characteristics
Five core dimensions of personality for brands
Sincerity
Excitement
Competence
Sophistication
Ruggedness

Aaker’s Brand Personality
Brands can strategically position themselves to excel in certain personality aspects

“Australian Made” brand perception

Consumer-brand relationships
When brands are humanised via human personality traits, this facilitates interactions and relationships with them
Consumers often hold similar feelings towards brands and people (e.g. interdependence, commitment, loyalty)
Consumers’ relationships with brands provide a channel for the exchange of information, meaning and identity

Part 4. Brand Extensions
A brand extension is a strategy to extend the business.

Brand extension categories
Line extensions = new products in the same category, under the same brand
Pepsi Max, KFC Hot Wings

Brand extension categories
Brand extensions = new products in a different/related category, under the same brand

Brand extension categories
Multibranding = new products in the same category, under different brands

Brand extension categories
New Brands = new products in different categories, under different brands

Branded House vs House of Brands
Line extension and Brand extensions are sometimes referred to as “Branded House” because they all use the same core brand name

Branded House vs House of Brands
Multibrands and New Brands are sometimes referred to as “House of Brands” because they use different brand names.

Assignment 4
Consider:
How can you convey your differentiation through brand elements?
What ‘personality’ should your brand have?
How can you leverage positive associations to build brand equity?
What branding extension strategy should you choose?

Assignment 4 - New brand extension for one of your target market
Your 3 personas from the first Assignment 1
Your individual brand report Assignment  2

The brand that you reported (Assignment 2)
The personas you created (Assignment 1)
For example, what new Brand Extension product will you create, for whom?
Analyse which of the three is most likely to be profitable
Select only one based on your analysis
Develop a brand extension based on your understanding of that segment’s behaviour
